title: Beyond ‘blood gold’: understanding the spectrum of illicit gold conflict
author: IISS
text:
Daniel Watson Research Associate, Conflict, Security and Development About Daniel

Online Analysis3rd July 2026

Beyond ‘blood gold’: understanding the spectrum of illicit gold conflictAs global demand for gold increases, so too does the need for a clearer understanding of whether illicit gold production and trafficking are linked to violent conflict.

The relationship between illicit gold and violent conflict is commanding increasing international attention and concern, amid high global demand for gold. The association of gold with conflicts in Sudan and the Sahel has led some to speak of ‘blood gold’, while Russia is reportedly recycling gold extracted from conflict-affected African states to finance its own war in Ukraine.
The high value, portability and easy convertibility of gold to cash (and increasingly, to cryptocurrencies) make gold an ideal medium of transaction for actors engaged in illicit activity. This includes organised criminal groups, non-state armed groups, and state or military actors trying to circumvent anti-money-laundering requirements or bypass legitimate supply chains. These illicit gold economies can expand across borders, spreading instability and creating transnational networks that carry gold to major intermediary and destination markets. Illicit gold is gradually blended with legitimate gold as it moves through these networks.
One way to make sense of conflicts linked to illicit gold is to place them on a spectrum, roughly divided into three segments or tiers, with the scale of conflict intensifying the further along the spectrum a country moves. At the lower end, conflicts tend to be relatively disorganised and localised, gradually becoming more organised as they move along the spectrum and draw in increasingly powerful actors. At the most extreme end, the power of these actors can only be maintained through the continued control of illicit gold, which becomes the foundation of a war economy that draws together rival actors and their allies. Understanding these dynamics can help anticipate which gold-producing countries are vulnerable to specific types of conflict, and under what circumstances.
Localised resource conflicts
At one end of the spectrum are countries with relatively low levels of illicit gold production, but where mining sites overlap with economically valuable or politically significant land. This overlap can spark land conflicts, and in some cases, new discoveries of gold will destabilise local power relations or widen divisions along ethnic or livelihood lines, particularly when resource ownership is contested or where gold production has negative environmental repercussions on agricultural or pasture land.
Whether relatively contained episodes of violence escalate into more intensive and widespread conflicts can depend on various context-specific factors. A key influence is the presence of more powerful national or regional actors – such as elites connected to ruling regimes or the regimes of neighbouring countries, or armed opposition groups – who may wish to insert themselves into localised conflicts. If these actors are motivated more by the desire to capture revenues than by the prospect of restoring peace, then conflict may deepen, potentially generating further grievances that worsen violence.
Alongside land disputes and power struggles, episodes of localised armed violence linked to illicit gold can take several other forms. In some cases, they can occur between informal miners seeking to access mining areas and security forces protecting large-scale industrial mining sites, such as in Ghana. In Papua New Guinea, inter-clan disputes over access to mining areas have converged with discontent over land-use agreements between certain clans and international mining firms, leading to volatility and violence. Illicit gold can also fuel disorder and lawlessness: in northwestern Nigeria – where state authority has withered as armed groups have multiplied – artisanal mining is becoming a focal point for predation by criminal ‘bandit’ groups.
Conflict financing through illicit gold
Towards the middle of the spectrum are cases of more conventional civil wars involving government forces fighting against insurgent groups, where illicit gold can be used to sustain the warring parties, though it is not usually the primary means through which war is financed. This can take several forms, including direct or indirect involvement in the production or trafficking of gold by belligerents; the taxation of gold and other resources at checkpoints; or profiting from the extortion or kidnapping of miners or traders.
Although illicit gold was linked to several (mainly African) conflicts during the Cold War and post-Cold War eras, it formed only a small part of the war economies that supported these conflicts. However, illicit gold played a more significant role in the Congolese civil wars of the late 1990s and early 2000s, where multiple local and regional players – notably Ugandan and Rwandan military forces – were closely involved in the extraction and taxation of gold to fund military operations or for financial gain.
After the 2008 financial crisis, the (partial) financing of conflict through illicit gold became more widespread as rising international gold prices encouraged expansion of production in countries with little formal mining activity. This included Colombia, where insurgent groups – alongside criminal actors – escalated their involvement in illicit gold trafficking in the early 2010s, with lasting consequences for post-conflict insecurity and ecosystems following the 2016 peace agreement. Control over the illicit gold trade has since become an objective for armed groups in Colombia and elsewhere in the region.
The conflict economy in the Central African Republic (CAR) also shifted gear in the mid-2010s, when artisanal miners and armed groups began to veer away from the diamond trade – which in the CAR was placed under international embargo in 2013 through the Kimberley Process – and towards illicit gold. Control over gold-production sites and transit routes became a driver of conflict between militias, insurgent groups and the military, with the latter backed by the Wagner Group and the Rwandan army. Meanwhile, flows of illicit gold are becoming ensnared in Myanmar’s wider war economy since its return to civil war in 2021 and are being captured by military authorities and rebel movements in the country.
Although some war economies are associated with relatively stable conflict dynamics, soaring illicit gold production has tended to destabilise, creating new axes of competition within and between conflict parties and external players. In parts of the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, for instance, competition over gold revenues has fragmented armed groups into smaller militias focused on controlling mines and trading routes, producing more localised and volatile patterns of conflict.
Gilded war economies
The final third of the spectrum is reserved for unstable, conflict-prone countries that are governed transactionally and where gold rushes are under way. This includes post-conflict or post-transition contexts, in which peace is precarious and resources are strained.
War erodes both institutions and social norms that can constrain violence or wealth accumulation, leaving post-conflict societies vulnerable to transactional forms of governance. During post-conflict gold rushes, powerful elites and rival factions may seize control of gold mines and stymie efforts to formalise trade, using gold revenues to consolidate power rather than support economic recovery or institutional rebuilding. High global demand for gold, combined with its relative ease of production, makes it an appealing source of finance for elites seeking to expand their influence or address cash-flow constraints.
One consequence of these dynamics is that mining-related disputes proliferate and escalate. Protests over environmental contamination linked to gold mining and processing can pit residents against armed factions involved in illicit mining, while the role of foreign businesses in controlling land or profits from the gold trade may galvanise discontent and low-level armed conflict. These conflicts are often deceptively localised and point to widening societal schisms, brewing political tensions or deepening military fragmentation that can feed into renewed large-scale conflict or heightened instability.
Whether a society relapses into civil war varies considerably. Côte d’Ivoire did not revert to large-scale violent conflict despite sharing vulnerabilities with other post-conflict gold producers, whereas Sudan has descended into a severe civil war. In Ethiopia’s Tigray region, meanwhile, a gold rush has coincided with worsening internal and regional instability, heightening the risk of renewed conflict.
The nature of the ruling regime and the structure of the political order largely explain these divergent outcomes. Militarised regimes that are internally factionalised and whose territorial control is fragmented are at particular risk of re-entering conflict as gold production soars.
If a regime with these characteristics becomes embroiled in civil war or a spreading insurgency – such as in Sudan or the Sahel – then a gilded war economy may emerge, in which gold becomes the central element as other sources of revenue recede in importance. Belligerents and their allies become increasingly dependent on controlling gold production and trade to sustain their power, generate foreign exchange for imports and maintain partnerships with key external states. Internal conflicts morph into regionalised ones as insurgents and opposition groups move into new areas to control critical transport corridors, and governments scramble to contain the spreading fire.
These gilded war economies are also a potential time bomb. Intensive gold production cannot be sustained indefinitely, and this may lead to further instability as production declines unless the war economy is rebalanced around more predictable sources of income.
Vulnerability to illicit-gold conflict
Understanding illicit-gold conflicts in this way helps shed light on the interplay between political, economic and security dynamics at different points on the spectrum. In localised resource conflicts, illicit gold economies increase the risk of conflict by destabilising local political structures. However, political and economic dynamics are brought into closer alignment when illicit gold is used to finance ongoing wars. In more extreme cases, political and security structures converge around the capture and continued production of illicit gold, which can, under the wrong circumstances, gradually wrench these same structures apart, ushering in an unstable war economy dominated by illicit gold flows.
Although these dynamics are distinct, there are shared vulnerabilities across the entire spectrum. These include the scale at which illicit gold production is increasing (with rapidly growing production levels closely associated with conflict); existing and unresolved fault lines that are likely to be widened or sharpened by increasing production or competition for illicit gold; and the presence of an infrastructure to discreetly move gold out of the producing country and into intermediary or destination markets where demand for gold is highest. When these vulnerabilities start to overlap, conflict may not be far behind.
The security, economic and governance repercussions of illicit gold are discussed further in the recent IISS report, Mapping Risk and Resilience in the Global Illicit Gold Economy, which was produced with the financial support of the World Gold Council.

AuthorDaniel Watson Research Associate, Conflict, Security and Development
